flags.message: This forward cell's case was strategically mooted mid-stream: per the May 18, 2026 letter on the SCOTUS docket, respondent voluntarily dismissed her claims against the General Dynamics petitioners, the remaining petitioners have class settlements in principle, and the petition is to be held in abeyance while General Dynamics plans to seek Munsingwear vacatur ('grant certiorari and vacate') once the case is moot. The eventual ground-truth label therefore hinges on order wording rather than cert-worthiness: a Munsingwear GVR maps to 'granted' under cert_signals.py while a mootness/Rule 46 dismissal maps to 'dismissed', so scoring for this cell will conflate merits-grant probability with vacatur-practice probability. Prediction made accordingly (modal outcome: Munsingwear GVR, labeled granted), but maintainers may want to decide whether Munsingwear dispositions should score as grants for cert-prediction purposes.

gaps.label: CourtListener MCP search by docket number (e.g. 25-34) did not surface the Supreme Court docket easily, requiring fallback to Google Web Search.
CourtListener did not surface the related Supreme Court docket through its search collections.

No pdftotext (or equivalent) on the runner: extracting the fetched supremecourt.gov PDF required hand-rolling a content-stream decoder, which produced slightly garbled text. A provisioned text extraction for late party letters (like the QP/petition/BIO extracts) or a PDF-to-text utility would help.
No provisioned brief in opposition was available because the response is not yet due.
Text of the provisioned docket's May 18 dismissal-related letter was not among the fetched documents.
The ranged corpus query could not reach the remote store because DNS resolution failed.
The ranged corpus query failed because the corpus-store hostname could not be resolved.
a statpack cut conditioned on call-for-response would directly price this case's strongest signal

fedcourts query has no free-text/topic vocabulary discovery; a citation query for Munsingwear priors returned nothing and cost 408 ranged GETs (~107 MB), an expensive way to learn the corpus lacks that cut.

fedcourts query matched zero priors on FAA/arbitration citations despite two attempts; a topic or free-text hook for cert-stage priors would make the corpus retrieval surface actually usable for issue-alike petitions

helpful.label: CourtListener opinion search confirmed the principal Fourth Circuit authorities.
Google Web Search was critical in discovering that the related petition (No. 25-34) was dismissed under Rule 46.1, completely altering the posture of this case.
The complete petition text made the alleged split and vehicle arguments assessable.
The petition and brief in opposition permitted direct comparison of the asserted split and vehicle objections.
The provisioned snapshot exposed the response request and complete procedural timeline.
The provisioned snapshot's ProceedingsandOrder entries carried the DocumentUrl for the decisive May 18 abeyance letter, so one targeted fetch resolved what the entry text alone left ambiguous.
The snapshot exposed both the CVSG and the later dismissal-related abeyance request.
The statpack supplied calibrated modern-cert, CVSG, circuit, Term, and paid-petition base rates.
The statpack supplied fee-class, circuit, relist, and CVSG calibration.
courtlistener dockets endpoint answered the key live question (companion petition 25-34 still pending, not denied) in one call

no tool reaches current SCOTUS docket entries for a *related* case: courtlistener has no entry-level SCOTUS data and supremecourt.gov 403s the fetcher, so relist/CVSG status of the companion petition (the single most predictive fact here) could not be confirmed
a statpack cut conditioned on call-for-response would directly price this case's strongest signal
helpful: statpack signal cuts (relist, CVSG, originating-circuit) made it easy to price the call-for-response and companion-case signals against the low cert base rate
provisioned petition.txt and questions-presented.txt were complete and clean, so no retrieval was needed for the case's own merits record
courtlistener dockets endpoint answered the key live question (companion petition 25-34 still pending, not denied) in one call
recent.notes: Forward-mode cell; ~13 retrieval calls. Each fedcourts query cost ~107 MB of ranged reads to return nothing, which feels expensive for a miss.

helpful: web_fetch tool was essential for reading the un-provisioned abeyance letter directly from the docket link in the snapshot.
recent.notes: The provided statpack base rates for CVSG were very useful context.

recent.notes: The provisioned record was sufficient to complete the prediction. The failed corpus query returned no priors; CourtListener MCP remained available for general legal context.

gaps: No pdftotext (or equivalent) on the runner: extracting the fetched supremecourt.gov PDF required hand-rolling a content-stream decoder, which produced slightly garbled text. A provisioned text extraction for late party letters (like the QP/petition/BIO extracts) or a PDF-to-text utility would help.

fedcourts query has no free-text/topic vocabulary discovery; a citation query for Munsingwear priors returned nothing and cost 408 ranged GETs (~107 MB), an expensive way to learn the corpus lacks that cut.
statpack has no cut for petitions that go moot / are held in abeyance pending settlement, which is the operative population here.
helpful: The provisioned snapshot's ProceedingsandOrder entries carried the DocumentUrl for the decisive May 18 abeyance letter, so one targeted fetch resolved what the entry text alone left ambiguous.
statpack CVSG and relist cuts gave a usable quantitative anchor before the case-specific mootness adjustment.
recent.notes: The event now turns less on cert-worthiness than on Munsingwear-vacatur practice; the disposition label the extractor assigns (GVR → granted vs dismissal) is the crux, so having cert_signals.py readable in-repo was genuinely useful.
tools_used: curl fetch of a supremecourt.gov docket PDF
